text: The number of 10-digit numbers such that the product of any two consecutive digits in the number is a prime number, is
shortExplanation: Consecutive digits must be (prime, 1) or (1, prime). Considering primes 2, 3, 5, 7 and the digit 1, the total count reaches 2^11 / 2.

text: The number of six digit numbers that can be formed from the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 & 7 so that digits do not repeat and the terminal digits are even is:
shortExplanation: There are 3 even digits (2, 4, 6). Choose 2 for ends (^{3}2 = 6) and arrange remaining 5 digits in middle 4 places (^{5}4 = 120). 6 * 120 = 720.

text: Number of positive integers which have no two digits having the same value with sum of their digits being 45, is
shortExplanation: The sum of digits 0-9 is 45. Any such number must use all digits 0-9 exactly once (except leading zero cases).

text: The number of signals that can be made with 3 flags each of different colour by hoisting 1 or 2 or 3 above the other, is:
If all the letters of the word 'QUEUE' are arranged in all possible manner as they are in a dictionary, then the rank of the word QUEUE is:
shortExplanation: Alphabetical order: E, E, Q, U, U. Words starting with E: 4!/2! = 12. Next starts with Q: QEEUU(13), QEUEU(14), QEUUE(15), QUEEU(16), QUEUE(17).

text: 8 chairs are numbered from 1 to 8. Two women & 3 men wish to occupy one chair each. First the women choose the chairs from amongst the chairs marked 1 to 4, then the men select the chairs from among the remaining. The number of possible arrangements is:
shortExplanation: $Women choose 2 from 4: ^{4}2. Men choose 3 from remaining 6: ^{6}3.$

text: Number of words that can be made with the letters of the word 'GENIUS' if each word neither begins with G nor ends in S, is:
shortExplanation: Total words (6!) - (Starts with G) - (Ends with S) + (Starts with G AND ends with S). 720 - 120 - 120 + 24 = 504.

text: 5 boys & 3 girls are sitting in a row of 8 seats. Number of ways in which they can be seated so that not all the girls sit side by side, is:
shortExplanation: $Total arrangements (8!) - (3 girls together: 6! * 3!). 40320 - (720 * 6) = 36000.$

text: 10 different letters of an alphabet are given. Words with 5 letters are formed from these given letters, then the number of words which have atleast one letter repeated is:
shortExplanation: $Total 5-letter words (10^5) - Words with no repetition (^{10}5). 100000 - 30240 = 69760.$

text: In a conference 10 speakers are present. If S1 wants to speak before S2 & S2 wants to speak after S3, then the number of ways all the 10 speakers can give their speeches with the above restriction if the remaining seven speakers have no objection to speak at any number is:
shortExplanation: The 3 speakers S1, S2, S3 can be arranged in 3! = 6 ways. Restrictions imply S1 < S2 and S3 < S2. Valid orders: (S1, S3, S2) or (S3, S1, S2). That's 2/6 = 1/3 of total ways.

text: 20 Passengers are to travel by a double decked bus which can accommodate 13 in the upper deck and 7 in the lower deck. The number of ways that they can be divided if 5 refuse to sit in the upper deck and 8 refuse to sit in the lower deck, is
shortExplanation: 8 must go up, 5 must go down. 7 passengers remaining. To fill upper (needs 5 more) or lower (needs 2 more): ^{7}2 = 21.

text: How many nine digit numbers can be formed using the digits 2, 2, 3, 3, 5, 5, 8, 8, 8 so that the odd digits occupy even positions?
shortExplanation: Odd digits {3,3,5,5} in 4 even positions: 4!/(2!2!) = 6. Even digits {2,2,8,8,8} in 5 odd positions: 5!/(2!3!) = 10. Total = 6 * 10 = 60.

text: The number of words that can be formed by using the letters of the word 'MATHEMATICS' that start as well as end with T, is
shortExplanation: Fix T at ends. Remaining: M, M, A, A, H, E, I, C, S (9 letters). Ways = 9! / (2! * 2!) = 362880 / 4 = 90720.

text: The number of permutations that can be formed by arranging all the letters of the word 'NINETEEN' in which no two E's occur together, is
shortExplanation: Arrange N, I, N, T, N (5 letters, 3 N's): 5!/3!. Place 3 E's in 6 gaps: ^{6}3. Result: (5!/3!) * ^{6}3.

text: The number of ways in which 6 red roses and 3 white roses (all roses different) can form a garland so that all the white roses come together, is
shortExplanation: Treat 3 white roses as one unit. Total units = 6 red + 1 white unit = 7. Circular arrangement = (7-1)! = 6!. White roses internal arrangement = 3!. Total = 720 * 6 / 2 (for garland) = 2160.

text: The number of ways in which 5 beads, chosen from 8 different beads be threaded on to a ring, is:
How many ways are there to seat n married couples (n >= 3) around a table such that men and woman alternate and each woman is not adjacent to her husband.
shortExplanation: This is a standard problem involving inclusion-exclusion on circular permutations for the 'Problème des Ménages'.

text: The number of ways in which 6 men and 5 woman can dine at a round table if no two woman are to sit together is given by
shortExplanation: $Arrange men first: (6-1)! = 5!. Place 5 women in 6 gaps: ^{6}5. Total = 5! * 6!.$

text: Out of 7 consonants and 4 vowels, how many words can be made each containing 3 consonants and 2 vowels?
In a place there are 37 straight lines of which 13 pass through point A and 11 pass through point B. No line passes through both. Number of intersection points is:
From six gentlemen and four ladies, a committee of five is to be formed. Number of ways if the committee includes at least one lady is
The number of diagonals that can be drawn by joining the vertices of an octagon is:
The number of ways in which one or more balls can be selected out of 10 white, 9 green and 7 blue balls is
A lady gives a dinner party to 5 guests from 9 friends. Number of ways if two friends will not attend together is
$The number of proper divisors of a^p b^q c^r d^s where a,b,c,d are primes is:$
shortExplanation: $Total divisors = (p+1)(q+1)(r+1)(s+1). Proper divisors exclude 1 and the number itself, so subtract 2.$

text: How many divisors of 21600 are divisible by 10 but not by 15?
A team of 8 students goes on an excursion in two cars (seats 5 and 4). Number of ways they can travel is:
9 students (2 from A, 3 from B, 4 from C). Ways to select 4 students with at least 1 from each section:
shortExplanation: $Total ways (^{9}4) - (Missing one or more sections). Calculation leads to 120.$

text: Number of ways 10 identical apples can be distributed among 6 children so each gets at least one:
The number of terms in a complete homogeneous expression of degree n in x, y and z is
shortExplanation: $Equivalent to non-negative integral solutions of a+b+c=n, which is (n+3-1)C(3-1) = (n+2)C2.$

text: Number of ways of selecting 5 coins from 3 each of Rs 1, 2, 5 (identical denominations) is:
shortExplanation: $Coefficient of x^5 in (1+x+x^2+x^3)^3. Manual counting of partitions of 5 with parts max 3.$

text: The number of ways in which 16 identical things can be distributed among 4 persons if each gets at least 3:
shortExplanation: $Give 3 to each first (12 used). 4 left to distribute to 4 people: (4+4-1)C(4-1) = ^{7}3 = 35.$

text: Nine hundred distinct N-digit numbers are to be formed using 6, 8 and 9 only. The smallest value of N is:
$Number of distinct terms in the expansion of (x+y+z+w)^50 is:$
$Number of natural solution of the equation x+y+z=102 is:$
A set contains (2n+1) elements. Subsets with at most n elements is 4096. Value of n is:
$The number of positive integral solutions of abc=30 is:$
shortExplanation: 30 = 2*3*5. Each prime can go to a, b, or c (3 ways). Total = 3 * 3 * 3 = 27.

text: In how many ways 3 letters can be posted in 4 letter-boxes, if all are not in the same box:
shortExplanation: $Total ways = 4^3 = 64. Ways where all in same box = 4. 64 - 4 = 60.$

text: $The exponent of 3 in 100! is$
m men and w women seated in a row so all men sit together. Number of ways is:
shortExplanation: $Treat m men as one block. Total units = w + 1. Arrange units: (w+1)!. Internal men arrangement: m!.$

text: If a polygon has 90 diagonals, the number of its sides is:
A library has n different books, p copies of each. Number of ways of selecting one or more books:
shortExplanation: $For each book, can choose 0 to p copies (p+1 ways). Total (p+1)^n. Subtract 1 for selecting zero books.$

text: Number of ways to get a score of 11 by throwing three dice is:
shortExplanation: $Coefficient of x^11 in (x+x^2...+x^6)^3. Calculated as 27.$

text: The number of ways we can arrange 4 letters of the word MATHEMATICS is:
shortExplanation: Case 1: 2 alike, 2 alike. Case 2: 2 alike, 2 diff. Case 3: All 4 diff. Summing these gives 2454.

text: Number of squares on a chessboard is:
The number of ways in which 10 candidates A1...A10 can be ranked so A1 is always above A2 is:
shortExplanation: $In total 10! arrangements, A1 is above A2 in half and below in half due to symmetry.$

text: The number of ways of arranging p positive and n (n <= p+1) negative signs so no two negative are together is:
shortExplanation: Arrange p '+' signs. There are p+1 gaps. Choose n gaps for '-' signs: (p+1)Cn.

text: Number of students in a class if they send greeting cards to each other and total delivered is 1640:
25 identical things distributed among 5 persons. Number of ways each receives at least one:
shortExplanation: $Using stars and bars: (25-1)C(5-1) = ^{24}4.$

text: The number of 5 digit numbers that contain 7 exactly once is:
shortExplanation: Case 1: 7 is first digit (9^4). Case 2: 7 is in other 4 places (4 * 8 * 9^3). Total leads to 41 * 9^3? PDF lists (41)(9^4) as an option.

text: The number of 10 digit numbers that can be formed by using the digits 2 and 3 is
shortExplanation: Each of the 10 positions can be filled in 2 ways (either by 2 or 3). Total ways = 2 * 2 * ... (10 times) = 2^10.

text: If ^{n}r stands for ^{n}r then sum of the series 1 + ^{1}1 + ^{2}2 + ^{3}3 + ...... + nPn is
shortExplanation: Using the property r.Pr = Pr+1 - Pr, the series telescopes to leave only the last term plus the initial adjustment, resulting in (n+1)P(n+1).

text: Rakshit is allowed to select (n+1) or more books out of (2n+1) distinct books. If the number of ways in which he may not select all of them is 63, then value of n is
shortExplanation: Total ways to select (n+1) or more is 2^(2n). Given 2^(2n) - 1 = 63 (excluding 'all' selection), 2^(2n) = 64, so 2n = 6 and n = 3.

text: The number of ways in which a committee of 3 women and 4 men be chosen from 8 women and 7 men if Mr. X refuses to serve on the committee if Mr. Y is a member of the committee, is
shortExplanation: Total ways (^{8}3 * ^{7}4) - Ways both serve (^{8}3 * ^{6}3 if X and Y are both men, adjust based on gender). Assuming X and Y are both men: 56 * 35 - 56 * 20 = 56 * 15 = 840. (Note: Specific values vary based on X/Y gender identity in problem).

text: The number of ways in which 5 different books can be distributed among 10 people if each person can receive at most one book and no person receives more than one book, is
shortExplanation: This is a permutation of 10 items taken 5 at a time. 10 * 9 * 8 * 7 * 6 = 30240.

text: The number of ways in which 10 different pearls can be arranged to form a necklace is
shortExplanation: $For circular permutations where clockwise and anti-clockwise are indistinguishable (like a necklace), the formula is (n-1)!/2.$

text: The number of six-digit numbers which have sum of their digits as an odd integer, is
shortExplanation: Total 6-digit numbers are 900,000. Exactly half will have an odd sum and half will have an even sum.

text: $The number of divisors of the form 4n+2 (n >= 0) of the number 240 is$
shortExplanation: 240 = 2^4 * 3^1 * 5^1. Divisors of form 4n+2 are 2 * (odd number). Odd divisors of 240 are 1, 3, 5, 15. Total = 4.

text: Number of ways in which 7 people can be seated in a row such that two particular people are always together is
shortExplanation: Treat the 2 people as one unit. Now there are 6 units to arrange (6!). The 2 people can swap places (2!).

text: The number of ways in which 5 people can be chosen from 10 such that two particular people are never together is
shortExplanation: Total ways to choose 5 minus the ways where both particular people are included (remaining 3 chosen from 8).

text: In a group of 15 people, everyone shakes hands with everyone else. Total number of handshakes is
shortExplanation: $A handshake occurs between 2 people. Total = ^{15}2 = (15 * 14) / 2 = 105.$

text: The number of ways to arrange the letters of the word 'CHESTNUT' such that all vowels are together is
shortExplanation: Vowels are {E, U}. Treat as one unit. Units: C, H, S, T, N, T, {EU}. 7 units with two T's: 7!/2!. Multiply by internal vowel arrangement 2!. Result: 7!.

text: Total number of 3-digit numbers in which all digits are different is
shortExplanation: 1st digit: 9 ways (1-9). 2nd digit: 9 ways (0-9 excluding 1st). 3rd digit: 8 ways. 9 * 9 * 8 = 648.

text: The number of ways in which 4 boys and 4 girls can sit around a circular table so that no two girls are together is
shortExplanation: Arrange boys first: (4-1)! = 3!. Place girls in the 4 gaps between boys: ^{4}4 = 4!. Total = 3! * 4!.

text: How many 4-digit numbers can be formed using the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 without repetition that are divisible by 5?
shortExplanation: The last digit must be 5 (1 way). The remaining 3 positions can be filled from 5 digits in ^{5}3 = 5 * 4 * 3 = 60 ways.

text: The number of ways to choose 4 cards from a pack of 52 cards such that they are of different suits is
shortExplanation: Choose 1 card from Spades (13), 1 from Hearts (13), 1 from Diamonds (13), and 1 from Clubs (13). 13 * 13 * 13 * 13 = 13^4.

text: Total number of ways in which 10 identical apples can be distributed among 3 children is
$If 10C_r = 10C_(r+2), then the value of r is$
shortExplanation: Properties of combinations: nCx = nCy implies x = y or x + y = n. Here r + r + 2 = 10, so 2r = 8, r = 4.

text: The number of ways of arranging the letters of the word 'BANANA' is
shortExplanation: Total letters = 6. Repetitions: A (3 times), N (2 times). Ways = 6! / (3! * 2!) = 720 / 12 = 60.

text: The number of ways to form a committee of 5 members from 6 men and 4 women such that it contains exactly 2 women is
shortExplanation: Choose 2 women from 4 (^{4}2 = 6) and 3 men from 6 (^{6}3 = 20). Total = 6 * 20 = 120.

text: How many diagonals are there in a decagon?
shortExplanation: $Formula for diagonals = n(n-3)/2. For n=10, 10(7)/2 = 35.$

text: The number of signals that can be generated by 5 different flags by hoisting any number of them is
A set has 5 elements. The number of non-empty subsets is
shortExplanation: $Total subsets = 2^n = 2^5 = 32. Non-empty subsets = 32 - 1 = 31.$

text: Number of 4-digit numbers that can be formed using digits {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5} with no repetition is
shortExplanation: 1st digit: 5 ways (1-5). Remaining 3 positions from remaining 5 digits: ^{5}3 = 60. Total = 5 * 60 = 300.

text: The number of ways to arrange 5 people in a queue such that A is always ahead of B is
shortExplanation: $Total arrangements are 5! = 120. In exactly half of these, A will be ahead of B.$

text: Number of ways of selecting at least one fruit from 5 apples, 4 oranges and 3 mangos (fruits of same species are identical) is
How many words can be formed by using all letters of the word 'MISSISSIPPI'?
A box contains 2 white, 3 black and 4 red balls. Number of ways 3 balls can be drawn such that at least one is black is
shortExplanation: $Total ways ^{9}3 = 84. Ways with no black balls ^{6}3 = 20. 84 - 20 = 64.$

text: The number of ways in which 12 identical coins can be put into 5 different purses if none of the purses remains empty is
shortExplanation: $Using formula (n-1)C(r-1) for non-empty distribution: (12-1)C(5-1) = ^{11}4 = 330.$

text: Number of parallelograms formed from a set of 4 parallel lines intersecting another set of 3 parallel lines is
The number of odd divisors of 1000 is
shortExplanation: 1000 = 2^3 * 5^3. Odd divisors are the divisors of 5^3. These are 5^0, 5^1, 5^2, 5^3. Total = 4.

text: How many 5-digit numbers are there which are palindromes?
$The exponent of 5 in 100! is$
The number of ways to arrange the letters of 'COCONUT' is
A committee of 3 is to be formed from 6 people. In how many ways can this be done if two particular people must be included?
shortExplanation: $Since 2 people are fixed, we only need to choose 1 more from the remaining 4. ^{4}1 = 4.$

text: Number of triangles that can be formed by joining 12 points in a plane, of which 5 are collinear is
shortExplanation: $Total triangles ^{12}3 = 220. Minus triangles from collinear points ^{5}3 = 10. 220 - 10 = 210.$

text: The number of ways of distributing 8 different balls in 3 different boxes is
shortExplanation: Each of the 8 balls has 3 choices of boxes. Total = 3 * 3 ... (8 times) = 3^8.

text: The number of ways to seat 5 men and 5 women at a round table such that men and women alternate is
shortExplanation: $Arrange men: (5-1)! = 4!. Arrange women in 5 gaps: ^{5}5 = 5!. Total = 4! * 5!.$

text: The sum of all 3-digit numbers formed using digits {1, 2, 3} without repetition is
shortExplanation: Each digit appears in each place 2! times. Sum = (1+2+3) * 2! * (100 + 10 + 1) = 6 * 2 * 111 = 1332.

text: The number of 4-letter words with or without meaning that can be formed from 'LOGARITHMS' if repetition is not allowed is
How many ways can 5 prizes be given to 4 boys, when each boy can receive any number of prizes?
shortExplanation: Each prize can go to any of the 4 boys. Total = 4 * 4 * 4 * 4 * 4 = 4^5 = 1024.

text: $The number of zero's at the end of 50! is$
The number of ways to select 3 distinct numbers from {1, 2, ... 100} such that they are in A.P. is
shortExplanation: For 3 numbers a, b, c to be in A.P., a+c must be even. Number of ways to choose 2 even numbers is ^{50}2, 2 odd numbers is ^{50}2. Total = 1225 + 1225 = 2450.

text: The number of ways to arrange 6 people in two rows of 3 is
shortExplanation: $This is a linear arrangement of 6 people. 6! = 720.$

text: The number of ways in which 3 people can be chosen from 10 if two particular people refuse to serve together is
shortExplanation: $Total ways ^{10}3 = 120. Minus ways both are together ^{8}1 = 8. 120 - 8 = 112.$

text: How many ways can the letters of 'APPLE' be arranged?
The number of ways to put 5 different letters into 5 addressed envelopes such that all are wrong is
shortExplanation: This is a derangement problem. D5 = 5!(1 - 1/1! + 1/2! - 1/3! + 1/4! - 1/5!) = 44.

text: In a room, there are 12 bulbs of the same wattage, each having a separate switch. The number of ways to light the room with different amount of illumination is
shortExplanation: Each bulb can be on or off (2 ways). Total ways 2^12. Subtract 1 for the case where all bulbs are off.

text: We have to choose a committee of 11 players from 15 players with specific conditions. The number of selections when a particular batsman and a particular wicket keeper do not want to play together is
shortExplanation: Based on the options and standard player selection logic, the specific value is calculated by subtracting the 'together' case from total possibilities.

text: Let 52 cards of a deck be arranged in a line. Number of ways in which spades appear in increasing order of denomination is
shortExplanation: Fix the positions of the 13 spades. There is only 1 way to order them increasingly. The remaining 39 cards can be arranged in 39! ways. Total = ^{52}13 * 39! = 52!/13!.

text: 60 words can be made using all the letters of the word BHBJO, with or without meaning. If these words are written as in a dictionary. then the 50th word is: [05 April, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Arrange letters alphabetically: B, B, H, J, O. Calculate permutations starting with each letter until reaching the 50th rank.

text: If all the words with or without meaning made using all the letters of the word 'NAGPUR' are arranged as in a dictionary, then the word at 315th position in this arrangement is: [06 April, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Alphabetical order of NAGPUR is A, G, N, P, R, U. Fix letters one by one and count arrangements until rank 315.

text: The number of five digit numbers, greater than 40000 and divisible by 5, which can be formed using the digits 0, 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9 without repetition, is equal to: [12 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: The first digit must be 5, 7, or 9. The last digit must be 0 or 5. Case-wise analysis for leading and terminal digits is required.

text: The total number of three-digit numbers, divisible by 3, which can be formed using the digits 1, 3, 5, 8, if repetition of digits is allowed, is: [15 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: A number is divisible by 3 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3. List possible combinations from {1, 3, 5, 8} and count their permutations.

text: Eight persons are to be transported from city A to city B in three cars of different makes. If each car can accommodate at most three persons, then the number of ways, in which they can be transported, is: [10 April, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: The possible distribution of 8 people in 3 cars (max capacity 3) is (3, 3, 2). Use multinomial coefficient for grouping and then arrange.

text: Number of 4-digit numbers (the repetition of digits is allowed) which are made using the digits 1, 2, 3 and 5, and are divisible by 15, is equal to: [29 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Divisible by 15 means divisible by 3 and 5. The last digit must be 5. The sum of the first three digits plus 5 must be a multiple of 3.

text: The number of arrangements of the letters of the word 'INDEPENDENCE' in which all the vowels always occur together is: [8 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Treat all vowels (I, E, E, E, E) as one block. Permute this block with the remaining 7 consonants while accounting for duplicates.

text: The number of integers, greater than 7000 that can be formed, using the digits 3, 5, 6, 7, 8 without repetition, is: [24 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Count all 5-digit numbers possible (5!) and 4-digit numbers starting with 7 or 8 (2 * ^{4}3). Sum both cases.

text: The number of numbers, strictly between 5000 and 10000 can be formed using the digits 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 without repetition, is: [25 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Numbers must be 4-digit. The first digit can be 5, 7, or 9 (3 options). Remaining 3 spots are 4 * 3 * 2. Total = 3 * 24 = 72.

text: The letters of the word OUGHT are written in all possible ways and these words are arranged as in a dictionary, in a series. Then the serial number of the word TOUGH is: [29 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Alphabetical order: G, H, O, T, U. Count words starting with G, H, O, then TH, TOH, TOG, until reaching TOUGH.

text: All words, with or without meaning, are made using all the letters of the word MONDAY. These words are written as in a dictionary with serial numbers. The serial number of the word MONDAY is: [13 April, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Alphabetical order: A, D, M, N, O, Y. Use the rank formula or count starting letters to find the position of MONDAY.

text: If the letters of the word MATHS are permuted and all possible words so formed are arranged as in a dictionary with serial numbers, then the serial number of the word THAMS is: [11 April, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Alphabetical order: A, H, M, S, T. Count all words starting with A, H, M, S (4 * 4!) and then T-words until THAMS.

text: The number of square matrices of order 5 with entries from the set {0, 1}, such that the sum of all the elements in each row is 1 and the sum of all the elements in each column is also 1, is: [24 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: $This problem is equivalent to finding the number of permutations of 5 elements (5!), which is 120.$

text: The total number of six digit numbers, formed using the digits 4, 5, 9 only and divisible by 6, is: [1 Feb, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Divisible by 6 means even and divisible by 3. Last digit must be 4. Sum of remaining 5 digits + 4 must be a multiple of 3.

text: Let S = {1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 11}. The number of non-empty subsets of S that have the sum of all elements a multiple of 3, is: [25 Jan, 2023 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Analyze remainders of elements mod 3. Use generating functions or case analysis to find combinations that sum to 0 mod 3.

text: The total number of 4-digit numbers whose greatest common divisor with 54 is 2, is: [29 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: GCD(n, 54) = 2 implies n is even, not divisible by 3, and not divisible by 27. Apply inclusion-exclusion on 4-digit range.

text: Let the digits a, b, c be in A.P. Nine-digit numbers are to be formed using each of these three digits thrice such that three consecutive digits are in A.P. at least once. How many such numbers can be formed? [12 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: $Use the multinomial formula to find total arrangements (9! / (3!3!3!)) and subtract cases where no A.P. sequence appears.$

text: A person forgets his 4-digit ATM pin code. But he remembers that in the code all the digits are different, the greatest digit is 7 and the sum of the first two digits is equal to the sum of the last two digits. Then the maximum number of trials necessary to obtain the correct code is: [15 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Identify sets of 4 distinct digits {a, b, c, d} from {0...7} where max is 7 and a+b = c+d. Count permutations for each set.

text: The number of 3-digit numbers, that are divisible by either 2 or 3 but not divisible by 7 is: [31 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Calculate numbers divisible by 2, by 3, and their intersection. Then subtract those divisible by 14, 21, and 42.

text: If 2n+1 P n-1 : 2n-1 P n = 11 : 21 then n^2 + n + 15 is equal to: [1 Feb, 2023 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Expand the permutation formula, simplify the ratio to find n, and then compute the required quadratic expression.

text: Total numbers of 3-digit numbers that are divisible by 6 and can be formed by using the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 with repetition, is: [13 April, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Last digit must be even (2 or 4). Sum of three digits must be divisible by 3. Count pairs for first two digits.

text: The number of 4-letter words, with or without meaning, each consisting of 2 vowels and 2 consonants, which can be formed from the letters of the word UNIVERSE without repetition is: [6 April, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Word UNIVERSE has vowels {U, I, E} and consonants {N, V, R, S}. Select (^{3}2 * ^{4}2) and arrange them in 4! ways.

text: A boy needs to select five courses from 12 available courses, out of which 5 courses are language courses. If he can choose at most two language courses, then the number of ways he can choose five courses is: [24 Jan, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Sum of cases: (0 language, 5 others) + (1 language, 4 others) + (2 language, 3 others). Use ^{5}r * 7C(5-r).

text: If all the six digit numbers x1x2x3x4x5x6 with 0 < x1 < x2 < x3 < x4 < x5 < x6 are arranged in the increasing order, then the sum of the digits in the 72th number is: [29 Jan, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Each number is a combination of 6 digits from {1-9}. Find the 72nd combination in lexicographical order.

text: The number of seven digit positive integers formed using the digits 1, 2, 3 and 4 only and sum of the digits equal to 12 is: [13 April, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Use multinomial theorem (coefficient of x^12 in (x+x^2+x^3+x^4)^7) or partitions of 12 into 7 parts using {1, 2, 3, 4}.

text: Let x and y be distinct integers where 1 \le x \le 25 and 1 \le y \le 25. Then, the number of ways of choosing x and y, such that x + y is divisible by 5, is: [25 Jan, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Group numbers by remainder mod 5. Pair (r1, r2) such that r1 + r2 = 0 or 5. Calculate combinations from each group.

text: Number of 4-digit numbers that are less than or equal to 2800 and either divisible by 3 or by 11, is equal to: [31 Jan, 2023 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Count multiples of 3 in [1000, 2800], multiples of 11, and subtract multiples of 33.

text: The number of ways to distribute 30 identical candies among four children C1, C2, C3 and C4 so that C2 receives atleast 4 and atmost 7 candies, C3 receives atleast 2 and atmost 6 candies, is equal to: [28 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: $Use multinomial expansion (coefficient of x^30) with constraints on C2 and C3, then apply the formula (n+r-1)C(r-1).$

text: The total number of 5-digit numbers, formed by using the digits 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7 without repetition, which are multiple of 6, is: [28 June, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Sum of all digits is 24 (divisible by 3). Remove one digit such that sum remains divisible by 3. Then ensure evenness.

text: Let S be the set of all passwords which are six to eight characters long, where each character is either an alphabet from {A, B, C, D, E} or a number from {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} with the repetition of characters allowed. If the number of passwords in S whose at least one character is a number is \alpha \times 5^6 then \alpha is equal to: [28 July, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: $Total passwords minus passwords with only alphabets. Calculate for lengths 6, 7, and 8, then divide by 5^6.$

text: In an examination, there are 5 multiple choice questions with 3 choices. Marks are: 3 for correct, -2 for wrong, 0 for unattempted. The number of ways a student gets 5 marks is: [24 July, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Let x, y, z be correct, wrong, unattempted. 3x - 2y = 5. Find integer solutions (x, y) where x+y \le 5, then use combinations.

text: The number of 3-digit odd number, whose sum of digits is a multiple of 7, is: [25 June, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Fix last digit as {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}. For each, find pairs (d1, d2) such that sum + last digit is 7, 14, or 21.

text: Ten boys and five girls are in a class. Then the number of ways of forming a group consisting of three boys and three girls, if both B1 and B2 together should not be the members of a group, is: [26 June, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: $Total groups (^{10}3 * ^{5}3) minus groups containing both B1 and B2 (^{8}1 * ^{5}3).$

text: The letters of the word 'MANKIND' are written in all possible orders and arranged in serial order as in an English dictionary. Then the serial number of the word 'MANKIND' is: [25 July, 2022 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Alphabetical: A, D, I, K, M, N, N. Count words starting with A, D, I, K, then MA, MAD, MAI, MAK until MANKIND.

text: The number of 5-digit natural numbers, such that the product of their digits is 36, is: [26 July, 2022 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Factorize 36 into 5 single digits (including 1s). Permute each valid set (e.g., {9, 4, 1, 1, 1}, {6, 6, 1, 1, 1}) and sum them.

text: The number of 7-digit numbers which are multiples of 11 and are formed using all the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 and 9 is: [24 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Difference between sum of digits at odd and even places must be 0 or 11. Sum of all digits is 31. Set up equations.

text: The number of ways, 16 identical cubes, of which 11 are blue and rest are red, can placed in a row so that between any two red cubes there should be at least 2 blue cubes, is: [27 June, 2022 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Use gap method. Place 11 blue cubes. There are 12 gaps. Let red cubes be x1, x2, x3, x4, x5 with gap constraints.

text: The total number of 3-digit numbers, whose greatest common divisor with 36 is 2, is: [26 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Number must be even, not divisible by 3, and not divisible by 4. Use range [100, 999].

text: The number of matrices of order 3 \times 3, whose entries are either 0 or 1 and the sum of all the entries is a prime number, is: [29 July, 2022 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: Sum of entries can be 2, 3, 5, or 7. Calculate ^{9}r for r ∈ {2, 3, 5, 7} and sum them.

text: The total number of four digit numbers such that each of the first three digits is divisible by the last digit, is equal to: [29 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Iterate last digit d from 1 to 9. For each d, the count is (floor(9/d) * (floor(9/d)+1)^2). Sum for all d.

text: Let b1b2b3b4 be a 4-element permutation from {1...100} such that either (b1,b2,b3) or (b2,b3,b4) are consecutive integers. The number of such permutations is: [29 June, 2022 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Count sets where first 3 are consecutive, add sets where last 3 are consecutive, and subtract the intersection where all 4 are consecutive.

text: The total number of three-digit numbers, with one digit repeated exactly two times, is: [25 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Cases: Digit repeated is 0, or non-zero. Accounts for leading zero constraints and permutations.

text: A scientific committee is to be formed from 6 Indians and 8 foreigners, which includes at least 2 Indians and double the number of foreigners as Indians. Then the number of ways is: [24 Feb, 2021 (Shift-1)]
shortExplanation: $Cases: (2 Indians, 4 foreigners), (3 Indians, 6 foreigners), (4 Indians, 8 foreigners). Use ^{6}r * ^{8}2r.$

text: Team A consists of 7 boys and n girls and Team B has 4 boys and 6 girls. Total 52 single matches between teams where boy plays boy and girl plays girl. Then n is: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: $Matches = (BoysA * BoysB) + (GirlsA * GirlsB). 52 = (7*4) + (n*6). Solve for n.$

text: In a room, there are 12 bulbs of the same wattage, each having a separate switch. The number of ways to light the room with different amount of illumination is: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: $Each bulb can be ON or OFF (2 options). Total 2^12 ways, minus the case where all are OFF.$

text: Let 52 cards of a deck be arranged in a line. Number of ways in which spades appear in increasing order of denomination is: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: Fixing the order of 13 specific cards means we divide the total permutations (52!) by the number of ways to arrange those 13 cards (13!).

text: The number of ways of choosing 10 objects out of 31 objects where 10 are identical and 21 are distinct is: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: Sum of selecting r from distinct and (10-r) from identical. Results in sum of ^{21}r from r=0 to 10, which is half of 2^21.

text: The number of ways in which 15 identical objects can be distributed among 6 people such that each person gets at least one object: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: $Using stars and bars formula (n-1)C(r-1) for at least one distribution: (15-1)C(6-1) = ^{14}5.$

text: Number of ways in which 12 different books can be tied up in 3 bundles one containing 2 and the other two bundles containing 5 different books: [Retrieved Context]
shortExplanation: Group 12 into sizes 2, 5, 5. Formula: 12! / (2! 5! 5!). Since two groups are same size, divide by 2!.

text: $The exponent of 3 in 100! is: [Retrieved Context]$
